one_liner: Hybrid cloud storage that connects on-premises environments to AWS cloud storage via File, Volume, or Tape gateway modes.
when_to_use: On-premises applications need access to cloud-backed storage (NFS/SMB file shares, iSCSI block volumes, or virtual tape)
Migrate tape backups to S3/Glacier without changing existing backup workflows
Extend on-premises storage capacity with cloud-backed volumes
Provide low-latency local cache for frequently accessed cloud data
when_not_to_use: Pure cloud workloads with no on-premises component (use S3 or EBS directly)
High-throughput HPC file systems (use FSx for Lustre)
Bulk one-time data migration (use Snow family or DataSync)
key_facts: File Gateway: NFS/SMB interface backed by S3. Each file stored as an S3 object. Supports AD authentication for SMB.
Volume Gateway - Stored: Full dataset on-premises with async snapshots to S3 as EBS snapshots. Best when you need low-latency access to the entire dataset locally.
Volume Gateway - Cached: Primary data in S3, frequently accessed data cached locally. Best when dataset is too large for on-premises but you need low-latency access to working set.
Tape Gateway: Virtual tape library (VTL) backed by S3 and S3 Glacier. Compatible with existing backup software (Veeam, NetBackup, etc).
All modes deploy as a VM appliance on-premises (VMware, Hyper-V, or hardware appliance).
Volume Gateway snapshots are stored as EBS snapshots and can be used to create EBS volumes for EC2.
pricing_summary: Per-gateway fee (~$125/month) plus S3/EBS storage costs. Data written through gateway to S3 incurs standard S3 pricing. No charge for data read from local cache.
common_misconceptions: Stored Volume is NOT the same as Cached Volume. Stored keeps the full dataset on-premises; Cached keeps only the hot data locally.
File Gateway does NOT replace EFS. File Gateway is for hybrid on-prem-to-cloud access; EFS is for cloud-native shared file systems.
Tape Gateway does NOT require physical tapes. It emulates a tape library for backup software.

one_liner: Transform S3 objects dynamically at retrieval time using Lambda functions with no storage duplication needed.
when_to_use: Dynamic data masking or redaction per caller identity
Format conversion at read time (XML to JSON, image resizing)
Enriching data with external sources on retrieval
Filtering or subsetting data based on requester context
when_not_to_use: Static transformations that should be done once (use S3 Batch Operations or lifecycle rules)
High-throughput streaming with strict latency requirements (Lambda adds per-request latency)
Transformations that produce large output objects repeatedly (consider pre-transforming and storing)
key_facts: No storage duplication: transforms happen at GET time via a Lambda function
Works with existing S3 GET operations through an Object Lambda Access Point
Lambda function receives the original object and returns the transformed version
Supports objects up to 60GB
Can return different results for different callers using the same S3 object
Configured by creating an Object Lambda Access Point that references a supporting S3 Access Point and a Lambda function
pricing_summary: S3 GET cost + Lambda invocation ($0.20/1M) + Lambda duration. No additional storage cost since objects are not duplicated.
common_misconceptions: NOT a batch processing service: it runs per-request at retrieval time
Does NOT modify the stored object: the original remains unchanged in S3
Does NOT require changes to existing S3 PUT workflows: only GET is intercepted

one_liner: Write-once-read-many (WORM) protection for S3 objects with Governance and Compliance retention modes.
when_to_use: Regulatory requirements mandate immutable records (SEC 17a-4, FINRA, HIPAA)
Protect against accidental or malicious deletion of critical data
Legal hold requirements for litigation or compliance
Ransomware protection for backup data
when_not_to_use: General-purpose versioning without immutability requirements (use S3 Versioning alone)
Data that needs frequent updates or overwrites
Short-lived temporary data
key_facts: Two retention modes: Governance and Compliance
Governance mode: most users cannot overwrite or delete, but users with s3:BypassGovernanceRetention permission CAN override. Good for testing or soft protection.
Compliance mode: NO ONE can delete or overwrite the object, not even the root account, until the retention period expires. This is true WORM.
Legal Hold: independent of retention period. Can be applied/removed by users with s3:PutObjectLegalHold. Object cannot be deleted while legal hold is active.
Requires S3 Versioning to be enabled on the bucket
Object Lock must be enabled at bucket creation time (cannot be added to existing buckets without creating a new bucket)
Retention period is set per-object, not per-bucket (though default retention can be set at bucket level)
pricing_summary: No additional cost beyond standard S3 storage. Object Lock is a free feature of S3.
common_misconceptions: Governance mode is NOT absolute immutability: privileged IAM users can override it. Only Compliance mode provides true WORM.
Object Lock does NOT prevent new versions from being uploaded. It prevents deletion or overwrite of specific object versions.
Legal Hold and Retention are independent: an object can have both, either, or neither.

service: Amazon FSx for Windows File Server
one_liner: Fully managed Windows-native shared file storage with SMB protocol, NTFS, and direct Active Directory integration.
when_to_use: Windows workloads need shared file storage with SMB protocol
Applications require NTFS features (ACLs, shadow copies, user quotas)
Need to join a file system to an on-premises Active Directory domain
Home directories, department shares, or CMS content stores for Windows users
Lift-and-shift of Windows file servers to AWS
when_not_to_use: Linux-only workloads needing NFS (use EFS)
HPC parallel file systems (use FSx for Lustre)
Object storage patterns (use S3)
Single-instance block storage (use EBS)
key_facts: Supports SMB 2.0/3.0 protocol natively with full NTFS compatibility
Can join directly to on-premises Active Directory over VPN or Direct Connect. Does NOT require AWS Managed Microsoft AD.
Also supports AWS Managed Microsoft AD and AD Connector for AD integration
Supports DFS Namespaces for grouping multiple file shares
Multi-AZ deployment option for high availability (automatic failover)
Supports Windows shadow copies (VSS) for user-driven file restores
Data deduplication reduces storage costs by 50-80% for typical file shares
Accessible from on-premises via VPN or Direct Connect
pricing_summary: Storage: ~$0.013/GB/month (SSD) or ~$0.013/GB/month (HDD). Throughput: included base throughput with option to provision more. Multi-AZ doubles the storage cost.
common_misconceptions: Does NOT require AWS Managed Microsoft AD ($1,100+/month). FSx can join on-premises AD directly over VPN/Direct Connect.
NOT just for EC2 Windows instances. Can be accessed from on-premises servers, Amazon WorkSpaces, and AppStream.
NOT the same as EFS. FSx is Windows/SMB-native; EFS is Linux/NFS-native. They serve different ecosystems.

one_liner: Object storage with storage classes, lifecycle policies, versioning, encryption, Transfer Acceleration, static website hosting, and VPC endpoints for private access.
when_to_use: Store and retrieve any amount of data (backups, logs, media, data lakes, static websites)
Static website hosting for HTML/CSS/JS/images with no server management
Data lake foundation for analytics with Athena, Redshift Spectrum, or EMR
Long-term archival with lifecycle transitions to Glacier storage classes
Cross-region or cross-account data sharing
when_not_to_use: POSIX file system semantics needed by applications (use EFS or FSx)
Block storage for EC2 instance boot volumes or databases (use EBS)
Frequently updated structured data with transactional access (use a database)
Low-latency shared file access from multiple EC2 instances (use EFS)
key_facts: Storage classes: S3 Standard, Intelligent-Tiering, Standard-IA, One Zone-IA, Glacier Instant Retrieval, Glacier Flexible Retrieval, Glacier Deep Archive. Each has different durability, availability, and cost tradeoffs.
S3 Intelligent-Tiering: automatically moves objects between frequent and infrequent access tiers based on usage patterns. Best for unknown or changing access patterns. No retrieval fees.

S3 Lifecycle policies: automate transitions between storage classes and object expiration. Apply to current versions, previous versions, or both. Critical when versioning is enabled to control storage growth.

S3 Versioning: keeps multiple versions of objects. Deletion creates a delete marker (recoverable). With versioning, lifecycle rules must target previous versions separately or storage grows indefinitely.
S3 Transfer Acceleration: uses CloudFront edge locations to speed uploads over long distances by 50-500%. Ideal for global data collection to a single bucket.
VPC Gateway Endpoint for S3: free, routes S3 traffic through AWS private network instead of the internet. No NAT gateway charges. Does not support cross-region access.
VPC Interface Endpoint for S3 (PrivateLink): enables private access from on-premises via VPN/Direct Connect, or cross-region. Has per-hour and per-GB charges.
Server-side encryption: SSE-S3 (default, AWS-managed keys), SSE-KMS (KMS customer-managed keys with audit trail and rotation), SSE-C (customer-provided keys).
S3 Batch Operations: perform bulk operations (copy, invoke Lambda, restore from Glacier) on billions of objects. Uses S3 Inventory manifests.
MFA Delete: requires MFA to delete object versions or change versioning state. Additional protection against accidental or malicious deletion.
Static website hosting: serves index and error documents directly from S3. Combined with CloudFront for HTTPS and global distribution.
Multipart upload: required for objects over 5 GB, recommended for objects over 100 MB. Enables parallel uploads and resume on failure.
pricing_summary: S3 Standard: ~$0.023/GB/month. Standard-IA: ~$0.0125/GB/month + retrieval fee. Glacier Deep Archive: ~$0.00099/GB/month. Transfer Acceleration: $0.04-0.08/GB on top of transfer. VPC Gateway Endpoint: free. PUT/GET requests: $0.005/1K PUT, $0.0004/1K GET (Standard).
common_misconceptions: S3 Intelligent-Tiering is NOT just for infrequent data. It handles both frequent and infrequent access automatically and is ideal when access patterns are unpredictable.
Gateway VPC Endpoints for S3 are FREE. You do NOT need a NAT gateway for EC2-to-S3 traffic in private subnets. Gateway endpoints eliminate data transfer charges through NAT.
S3 Versioning does NOT automatically clean up old versions. Without lifecycle rules on previous versions, storage grows indefinitely as every overwrite and delete creates a new version.
S3 Transfer Acceleration is NOT the same as CloudFront. Transfer Acceleration speeds uploads TO S3; CloudFront speeds downloads FROM S3 (or any origin) via edge caching.
S3 Standard is already multi-AZ (3+ AZs). You do NOT need cross-region replication for AZ-level resilience within a region.
Default encryption (SSE-S3) is now enabled automatically on all new buckets. You do NOT need to configure it manually for basic encryption at rest.

one_liner: Fully managed, elastic NFS file system for Linux workloads that can be shared across multiple EC2 instances and Availability Zones simultaneously.
when_to_use: Multiple EC2 instances need concurrent read/write access to the same files (shared content, CMS, home directories)
Workloads that need a POSIX-compliant file system (Linux applications expecting standard file I/O)
Storage that must grow and shrink automatically without provisioning capacity
Container workloads (ECS, EKS, Fargate) needing persistent shared storage
Replacing instance store or EBS when data must survive instance termination and be shared
when_not_to_use: Windows workloads needing SMB/NTFS (use FSx for Windows File Server)
Single-instance high-IOPS block storage (use EBS io2 or gp3)
Object storage or data lake patterns (use S3)
HPC parallel workloads needing high throughput (use FSx for Lustre)
key_facts: NFS v4.0/v4.1 protocol. Works natively with Linux EC2 instances, ECS, EKS, and Fargate.
Elastic: automatically grows and shrinks as files are added/removed. No capacity provisioning needed.
Multi-AZ by default: data is stored redundantly across multiple AZs in a region for high availability and durability.
Storage classes: Standard (frequent access) and Infrequent Access (IA). Lifecycle management automatically moves files to IA after a configurable period (7, 14, 30, 60, or 90 days).
One Zone storage classes available for cost savings (~47% cheaper) when multi-AZ resilience is not required.
Performance modes: General Purpose (low latency, default) and Max I/O (higher aggregate throughput, higher latency).
Throughput modes: Bursting (scales with file system size), Provisioned (set throughput independent of size), Elastic (automatically scales throughput).
Supports encryption at rest (KMS) and in transit (TLS). Encryption at rest can be enabled at creation time.
Can be mounted from on-premises via AWS Direct Connect or VPN.
EFS Access Points: application-specific entry points that enforce a user identity and root directory for each application.
pricing_summary: Standard storage: ~$0.30/GB/month. Infrequent Access: ~$0.016/GB/month + $0.01/GB data access. One Zone: ~$0.16/GB/month. One Zone-IA: ~$0.0133/GB/month. No minimum fee or setup cost.
common_misconceptions: EFS is NOT the same as EBS. EBS is block storage attached to a single EC2 instance; EFS is a shared file system accessible from multiple instances simultaneously.
EFS is NOT for Windows. It uses NFS protocol (Linux). For Windows/SMB workloads, use FSx for Windows File Server.
EFS does NOT require capacity provisioning. It is elastic and grows/shrinks automatically. You pay only for what you store.
EFS Infrequent Access can reduce costs significantly (up to 92% cheaper than Standard) for files not accessed regularly. Enable lifecycle policies.
